verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Before I enter upon the subject matter of this passage, I must stand in admiration of words worthy of Theophrastus:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I am informed, he says, that some stir has been made on the question of the nature of the soul.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Whether complaints on a matter of this kind ought to be entertained instead of being put aside, you must yourself decide.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If these questions as to the origin of the soul have been stirred at Rome, what is the meaning of this complaint and murmuring on the question whether they ought to be entertained or not, a question which belongs entirely to the discretion of bishops?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But perhaps he thinks that question and complaint mean the same thing, because he finds this form of speech in the Commentaries of Caper.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then he writes: “Some of those whom I have read hold that the soul is infused together with the material body through the channel of the human seed; and of these they give such proofs as they can.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What license have we here in the forms of speech!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What mixing of the moods and tenses!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I have read some sayings—they confirmed them with what assertions they could.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And in what follows: “Others assert that God is every day making new souls and infusing them into the bodies which have been framed in the womb; while others again believe that the souls were all made long ago when God made all things of nothing, and that all that he now does is to send out each soul to be born in its body as seems good to him.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Here also we have a most beautiful arrangement.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Some, he says, assert this and that; some declare that the souls were made long ago, that is, when God made all things of nothing, and that He now sends them forth to be born in their own body as it pleases him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He speaks so distastefully and so confusedly that I have more trouble in correcting his mistakes than he in writing them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: At the end he says: “I, however, though I have read these things;” and, while the sentence still hangs unfinished, he adds, as if he had brought forward something fresh: “I, however, do not deny that I have both read each of these things, and as yet confess that I am ignorant.”
